summary: The Phase-1 artifact realizes the three-pillar architecture coherently: content-addressed determinism is end-to-end (CIDv1 + BLAKE3 + DAG-CBOR), handlers are subgraphs of OperationNodes walked by an iterative evaluator (code-IS-graph preserved), and DAG + bounded-iteration guarantees hold through registration-time invariant checks. The 6-crate layering is sound in direction (core<-graph<-{ivm,caps}<-eval<-engine) and is enforced by the absence of reverse-direction non-dev deps. PrimitiveHost is the correct composition seam — Engine implements it so benten-eval remains ignorant of benten-graph's concrete RedbBackend while still receiving a live backend at dispatch time. However, several smells warrant closure in Phase 2 before the public surface widens further: (1) benten-eval's EvalError wraps benten_graph::GraphError, dragging a redb-transitive dep into a crate the thin-engine test contract says must be optional; (2) the Phase-1 thinness story is runtime-config, not compile-feature, so `without_ivm()` / `without_caps()` still link every optional crate; (3) dispatch-time host buffering with per-call pending_ops + out-of-band transaction replay introduces a partial-failure shape (the evaluator returns Ok, then the replay Err-routes to ON_DENIED) that is coherent but subtle enough to deserve an explicit state diagram before more primitives are wired; (4) redb-error→String coercion in GraphError drops source() chain (R4b-tracked but still live); (5) 19 ignored tests are well-fenced with TODO(phase-2-*) markers and the boundaries they draw are the right ones.

findings.claim: benten-eval takes a non-dev dependency on benten-graph solely to re-export GraphError in EvalError::Graph, forcing every consumer of the evaluator to link redb transitively.
findings.detail: The EvalError enum in benten-eval carries `Graph(#[from] benten_graph::GraphError)`, but the evaluator's runtime path never constructs this variant itself — host-side failures surface via EvalError::Backend(String) or EvalError::Capability. The only effect of the #[from] is to make the engine's Engine::dispatch_call_inner able to write `let graph_err: GraphError = ...; Err(graph_err.into())`, which is a convenience for benten-engine not a need of benten-eval. Cost: benten-eval's dep graph pulls redb 4.x + 2 MB of storage-layer code into any downstream crate that wants just the evaluator + invariant battery (a use case docs/ARCHITECTURE.md names: 'evaluator-as-library'). This is a minor version of the same smell Phase-1 fixed on benten-caps (GrantReader trait, so benten-caps doesn't pull benten-graph).
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the benten-graph non-dev dep in benten-eval; drop EvalError::Graph; map graph errors at the engine boundary (eval_error_to_engine_error) into EngineError::Graph directly. Phase-2 item — no Phase-1 test exercises the EvalError::Graph #[from] path (grep confirms). Low-risk since EvalError::Backend(String) is the actual host-failure conduit.

findings.claim: Dispatch-time two-phase write pattern (evaluator returns projected CIDs from a pending_ops buffer, then a post-walk transaction replays them) is a coherent but under-documented state machine with three distinct failure surfaces that the Outcome mapping partially hides.
findings.detail: The evaluator walks the subgraph; PrimitiveHost::put_node pushes to ActiveCall.pending_ops and returns a projected CID so the evaluator's StepResult is well-formed. After Evaluator::run succeeds, the engine opens a transaction and replays pending_ops. Three failure modes: (a) evaluator fails mid-walk — no writes, clean EvalError path; (b) evaluator Ok, replay fails on capability — mapped to Outcome{edge:ON_DENIED, error_code}; (c) evaluator Ok, replay fails on SystemZoneWrite — mapped to Outcome{edge:ON_ERROR, E_SYSTEM_ZONE_WRITE}. Each surface is correct individually but the invariant 'a walk that called put_node 3 times and terminated successfully may still produce an Outcome with created_cid=None' is not surfaced in the Outcome docstring. Also: PrimitiveHost::put_node returns a projected CID but the eventual tx may reject, so any subgraph that reads back its own written CID within the same call (Phase-2 use case) will see a CID that never landed. Phase-1 has no such test; Phase-2 must add the invariant 'within a single dispatch_call, PrimitiveHost::put_node returns a CID that is eventually committed or the walk surfaces a typed error' or switch to write-through (transaction opened at dispatch entry, evaluator writes via tx).
findings.suggested_fix: Document the two-phase commit contract explicitly in dispatch_call_inner's docstring + Outcome's docstring. Add a Phase-2 TODO to convert to write-through once ITERATE + BRANCH primitive dispatch is wired (the buffering exists to defer cap-hook firing; with write-through the cap hook fires per primitive, which is what Invariant 13 wants anyway).

findings.claim: GraphError::Redb(String) stringifies every redb error variant and six `impl From<redb::*Error>` blocks drop the source() chain, limiting operator diagnostics.
findings.detail: R4b tracks this (GraphError::Redb docstring). The compromise is known. Architecturally the risk is: as benten-graph grows (Phase-2 async mirror + Phase-3 iroh fetch backend), the flat String representation makes it harder to programmatically route recovery (e.g., distinguishing 'redb::StorageError::ValueTooLarge' from 'redb::DatabaseError::UpgradeRequired' requires parsing the Display). The `#[from] redb::Error` refactor is test-surface-blocked (R3 tests construct GraphError::Redb('...') as injected errors), which is a classic test-couples-implementation smell.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2 work: introduce `GraphError::Redb { kind: RedbErrorKind, source: redb::Error }` where RedbErrorKind is a slim enum over the redb variants benten-graph actually cares about. Migrate R3 test fixtures to use a constructor (`GraphError::redb_injected(kind)`) so the refactor doesn't break the test surface. Already on the Phase-2 roadmap — surfacing it as an R6 finding to flag the architectural debt.

findings.claim: Thin-engine (without_ivm / without_caps) is a runtime config, not a compile-time feature. Every Phase-1 engine build links benten-ivm, benten-caps, and benten-eval whether or not they are used.
findings.detail: CLAUDE.md 'thinness test' reads as: "A developer should be able to use benten-core + benten-graph + benten-engine with NoAuthBackend, versioning disabled, and no IVM subscribers — and get a pure content-addressed graph database." The engine_builder_thinness.rs integration test enforces the runtime configuration surface (ivm_subscriber_count() == 0, grant_capability errors), which is the right Phase-1 choice — gating via cargo features right now would fracture the test matrix. But the binary/wasm size story doesn't match the principle. Phase-2 decision point: keep the orchestrator approach (unconditional links, runtime knobs) and accept the size tax, or introduce feature flags (`benten-engine/features = ['ivm', 'caps']`) and wrap the impls in cfg blocks. For embedded / WASM targets the latter matters.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: add cargo features on benten-engine ('ivm', 'caps', 'diag') and cfg-gate the Arc<Subscriber> field, the ivm submodule wiring in assemble(), and the PolicyKind::UCAN enum variant. Keep the public API stable — without_ivm() with 'ivm' feature disabled becomes a no-op (the field is already None). The thinness integration tests continue to pass unchanged; a new test matrix exercises feature-gated build profiles.

findings.claim: View-id recognition for read_view is a hard-coded whitelist of five canonical ids plus two prefix rules; Phase-1 does not gate Engine::create_view by allowlist, so users can register a view_id that read_view will then reject with E_UNKNOWN_VIEW.
findings.detail: `is_known_view_id` names the five Phase-1 view kinds; the IVM subscriber stores whatever view_id it was handed in create_view. A caller who `create_view('my_custom_view', opts)` and then `read_view('my_custom_view')` hits the `is_known_view_id` false path → UnknownView error, even though the subscriber has a live view registered. The code partially handles this via `ivm.view_is_stale(normalized)` probe *before* is_known_view_id, but the probe only returns Some() when the view exists and is_known_view_id is only reached when it does not — so the actual paths align in practice. Still: the whitelist exists as a separate source of truth from the subscriber's live set, which will drift when Phase-2 user-defined views ship.
findings.suggested_fix: Collapse is_known_view_id into `self.ivm.view_ids().contains(view_id) || is_phase1_canonical(view_id)`. The Phase-1 canonical set covers the auto-registered 'content_listing' fallback path when IVM is disabled; user-defined views route through the subscriber. Alternatively: drop the whitelist entirely and require create_view() to precede every read_view() — cleaner but breaks the current R3 test contract that reads canonical ids without first creating them.

findings.claim: Auto-registration of the 'content_listing' view for label 'post' at builder assemble-time + ad-hoc registration for other labels at register_crud-time is a hidden coupling between the zero-config DSL and the IVM subsystem.
findings.detail: EngineBuilder::assemble() unconditionally registers a ContentListingView for label 'post' whenever IVM is enabled. register_crud(label) then conditionally registers a ContentListingView for any non-'post' label. This produces the observable 'content_listing' view for the post fixture without a create_view() call — a Phase-1 DX win, but it couples the orchestrator assembly to a specific hand-written IVM view. A cleaner seam: have the engine publish a `register_default_views` hook that the DSL or the scaffolder invokes explicitly. Phase-2 risk: user-defined IVM views that also key off label 'post' but with different sort semantics will conflict with the auto-registered instance.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: move the auto-registration into an opt-in `EngineBuilder::auto_register_default_views()` method (default-on for Phase-1 ergonomics, explicit-off for power users). Alternatively bake into create_benten-app's scaffolded Engine::open() wrapper. Either way, decouple the orchestrator from a specific view-class identity.

findings.claim: Two parallel transaction types (`benten_graph::Transaction` owned by the storage layer, `benten_engine::EngineTransaction` wrapping it via a `GraphTxLike` object-safe shim) duplicate API surface and risk drift.
findings.detail: `benten_graph::Transaction<'tx>` owns the redb WriteTransaction + pending-ops buffer and exposes put_node/put_edge/delete_*. `benten_engine::EngineTransaction<'tx,'coll>` wraps it to add capability-hook collection. The wrapping is done via an object-safe trait (`GraphTxLike`) that elides the lifetime — a reasonable Phase-1 move to avoid threading the `'tx` lifetime through the closure passed to Engine::transaction. But now there are two transaction types users see in docs (benten-graph's is public), and the engine's version silently drops edge ops (the pending ops collector only tracks PutNode / DeleteNode; PutEdge / DeleteEdge are noted as 'reserved for Phase-2' in PendingHostOp). Users who call `backend.transaction()` directly (bypassing the engine) see one API; users who call `engine.transaction()` see a similar-looking but different one.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: make benten_graph::Transaction crate-private (only exposed via RedbBackend::transaction closure), so the only public transaction type is EngineTransaction. This also lets the engine own the ChangeEvent attribution fields (actor_cid / handler_cid / capability_grant_cid) which are currently Optional because the graph layer doesn't know them. Related to the R4b-tracked change_stream.rs:61 ignored test.

findings.location: crates/benten-graph/src/store.rs:257-297 (ChangeEvent) + mini-review history (G5 fix-pass widened payload)
findings.claim: ChangeEvent carrying `Option<Node>` and `Option<(Cid, Cid, String)>` edge_endpoints widens the graph-layer payload with content that IVM views need, and the boundary between 'what the graph emits' vs 'what IVM subscribers derive' is no longer crisp.
findings.detail: The G5 fix-pass widened ChangeEvent to carry the full Node body on Node events and edge endpoints on Edge events because the five hand-written IVM views (capability_grants needs `grantee`, content_listing needs `createdAt`, version_current needs `anchor_id`, governance_inheritance needs edge endpoints) couldn't derive their state from the identity-only event. This was the right pragmatic call for Phase-1, but it means ChangeEvent is now a 'view feed' more than a 'write notification' — every subscriber pays the Node-clone cost whether they use it or not. The ChangeBroadcast fan-out takes a &ChangeEvent so the cost is a single copy per commit, not per-subscriber; still, the semantic shift is real. A Phase-2 pull-based API (subscriber asks graph layer: 'give me the node body for this cid' via snapshot) would restore the crisp boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: add `ChangeEventMeta` (cid + labels + kind + tx_id) as the thin payload and keep ChangeEvent as the 'fat' variant, emitted only when at least one subscriber declares it needs property data. Alternatively expose a snapshot handle on ChangeEvent that subscribers can cheaply resolve into a Node lookup at the commit's redb read-tx. R6 note: this is pre-mature optimization at Phase-1 scale (5 views × 1 Node clone per commit is negligible); flag it for Phase-2 when view count grows.

findings.claim: Change-event observation queue (EngineInner.observed_events + event_count) is a process-wide list drained by offset — functional but coupled to stdlib Mutex<Vec> and bounded only by caller drain cadence.
findings.detail: Probes drain events ≥ start_offset; un-drained events stay forever. A test that creates a probe, commits 10,000 events, then never drains leaks 10,000 ChangeEvents (each carrying a full Node on Created). `subscribe_change_events` is test-shape-only at Phase-1 (the probe is for R3 assertion surfaces), so the leak is bounded by test lifetime. But `test_subscribe_change_events_matching_label` is #[cfg(any(test, feature='test-helpers'))] and the feature is default-on — downstream consumers (Thrum migration, Phase-2 DSL subscribe API) may pin a probe and forget. The drain_events_from logic is also O(n) per call, not O(k) where k=matching events, since `retain` walks the whole vec.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: either (a) implement a capped ring-buffer with oldest-event-eviction, or (b) swap to tokio::sync::broadcast on native + synchronous Vec on WASM (the change.rs docstring already names this). Document the probe's drain-cadence expectation; feature-gate test_subscribe_* behind a non-default 'test-helpers' feature so production consumers don't accidentally pin probes.

findings.claim: register_crud synthesizes a READ→RESPOND subgraph that is the *registered* shape, but dispatch_call_inner builds a *different* op-specific subgraph (WRITE→RESPOND for create, READ→RESPOND for list/get, WRITE→RESPOND for delete) at call-time. The handler CID stored at registration does not match the actual walk shape.
findings.detail: At register_crud('post'): the builder produces `SubgraphBuilder::new('crud:post').read('crud_post_read').respond(r)`. Its CID is `cid_registered`. At call_('post:create'): subgraph_for_crud synthesizes a NEW subgraph `SubgraphBuilder::new('crud:post:create').write(...).respond()`. Its CID is different — call it `cid_walked`. The handler table maps 'crud:post' → `cid_registered`, but the Evaluator actually walks `cid_walked`. This is correct for Phase-1 (the test contract asserts per-op primitive lists via trace(), not per-handler CID stability) but breaks Invariant 10 (content-addressed hash per subgraph) if a reviewer interprets 'the subgraph the engine dispatches' as 'the subgraph whose CID is the handler's identity'. Compromise #8 (documented closed: the evaluator is the sole dispatch path) holds structurally; the handler-id → subgraph-cid mapping is a separate concern. Still, a reader debugging `cid_walked != cid_registered` will find no documentation explaining why.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring on register_crud naming the Phase-1 compromise: 'The registered CID records the handler identity; the call-time evaluator walks an op-specific shape synthesized fresh per dispatch. Phase-2 replaces this with a multi-entry-point SubgraphSpec where each op is a registered branch from the shared root.' Consider renaming: handler_id → entry_id, and pinning the CID to the top-level 'crud:post' node that holds per-op sub-subgraphs.

findings.claim: All 8 named compromises (7 in plan + the closed #8 on eval-dispatch short-circuit) are documented, tested, and bounded in scope. None is a ticking time bomb at Phase-1; two (#1 TOCTOU, #2 existence-leak) have security surface that Phase-3 should close before sync ships.
findings.detail: Compromise #1 (TOCTOU window on long ITERATE): concrete 100-iter default, policy-overridable via iterate_batch_boundary(). Phase-2 Invariant 13 tightens. Tracked. OK. Compromise #2 (E_CAP_DENIED_READ leaks existence): named in SECURITY-POSTURE.md, regression test pins the prose. Phase-3 when sync/federation lands must choose Option A/B/C. OK for Phase-1. Compromise #3 (benten-errors inside benten-core): revisit Phase-2 if coupling surfaces; currently not. OK. Compromise #4 (WASM runtime Phase-2, BrowserOriginCapBackend deferred): structurally fine — the SANDBOX primitive type exists but executor errors PrimitiveNotImplemented. OK. Compromise #5 (per-capability write rate limits): recorded as metric only; Phase-3 enforces. OK — no Phase-1 attack surface. Compromise #6 (BLAKE3 128-bit collision bound): named in SECURITY-POSTURE.md; the whole architecture rests on this, correctly documented. Compromise #7 (bin gating for write-canonical-and-exit): purely build-hygiene, zero runtime impact. OK. Compromise #8 (closed): dispatch short-circuits — removed; the evaluator is the sole path now. The set is clean.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-3 explicitly revisits Compromise #1 (TOCTOU per-op) and #2 (existence-leak) before sync mesh opens. Add a checklist entry in the Phase-3 kickoff doc.

findings.claim: PrimitiveHost is the correct composition seam and is object-safe by construction, but it has 13 methods, some of which (iterate_batch_boundary, emit_event) are Phase-2-shaped stubs. The trait surface will grow as Phase-2 primitives land.
findings.detail: PrimitiveHost is the single abstraction that lets benten-eval dispatch every primitive without knowing about benten-graph or benten-caps concretely. Send + Sync + object-safe + no generic methods. The shape is good. Two observations: (1) iterate_batch_boundary() has a default impl returning 100 — this couples the trait to named-compromise #1 at the trait level, which is appropriate for Phase-1 but means the trait carries a workaround in its shape. (2) read_view takes a &ViewQuery defined in benten-eval, not a benten_ivm::ViewQuery — the right call for thinness, but the engine's impl PrimitiveHost::read_view (lib.rs:2118-2135) ignores the ViewQuery entirely and calls self.read_view(view_id) without query routing. Phase-2 must wire the ViewQuery through or the trait method is aspirational.
findings.suggested_fix: Phase-2: split PrimitiveHost into smaller composable traits (NodeHost / CapabilityHost / ViewHost / IterationHost) per ENGINE-SPEC §6.2; the Evaluator takes `&dyn PrimitiveHost` bound by all of them, and test hosts can mix-and-match. Document that iterate_batch_boundary's default returning 100 IS compromise #1's enforcement knob.

positive_observations: Content-addressing is end-to-end: Node::cid / Edge::cid / Subgraph::cid all route through the same BLAKE3+DAG-CBOR path, anchor_id is #[serde(skip)] so version-chain identity does not affect CID, and the canonical fixture CID has been stable across R3-R5.

Code IS graph is preserved: Engine::register_subgraph takes a SubgraphSpec/Subgraph and runs the G6 invariant battery (1/2/3/5/6/9/10/12) before acceptance; Engine::call dispatches through Evaluator::run(&subgraph, &dyn PrimitiveHost), closing Compromise #8.

DAG + bounded iteration: Invariant 1 (DAG-ness via DFS coloring), Invariant 2 (max depth), Invariant 5/6 (node/edge caps), plus runtime iteration budget (DEFAULT_ITERATION_BUDGET=100_000 with E_INV_ITERATE_BUDGET). Turing-completeness escape hatch (SANDBOX) is a registered primitive type with PrimitiveNotImplemented at call-time — the structural constraint is live, not aspirational.

6-crate layering is correctly directional: no reverse-direction non-dev deps (verified via Cargo.toml inspection). The benten-caps::GrantReader trait is a model example of how to avoid the benten-caps→benten-graph layering break.
PrimitiveHost is object-safe + Send + Sync + no generic methods — correct for Phase-2 when iroh / WASM hosts ship. Evaluator takes &dyn PrimitiveHost and stays ignorant of the concrete backend.

Thinness test contract is encoded as an executable integration test (engine_builder_thinness.rs) with 'honest-no' error paths — without_caps rejects grant_capability, without_ivm rejects read_view — not silent no-ops.

Named compromises are all load-bearing documented in code + tests + SECURITY-POSTURE.md. The regression pattern (option_a_existence_leak_is_documented_compromise greps SECURITY-POSTURE.md) is idiomatic.

19 ignored tests all carry TODO(phase-2-*) or TODO(phase-3-*) markers naming a specific API surface (grant-backed-policy / version-chain-ivm / hlc / ivm-replay) — the Phase-1→Phase-2 boundary is drawn at API surfaces, not at test IDs, which is the right discipline.
ChangeBroadcast correctly lives in benten-engine::change rather than benten-graph (per R1 architect decision) — no tokio in benten-graph's dep graph.
notes: Verdict: accept-with-minors. The major findings (arch-1 redb-transitive via EvalError::Graph; arch-2 two-phase dispatch surface) are Phase-2 follow-ups, not Phase-1 blockers. The minor findings are a mix of architectural debt (arch-3 redb-error stringification, arch-4 runtime-thinness vs compile-feature-thinness, arch-7 duplicated Transaction types) and pre-mature-optimization flags (arch-8 ChangeEvent payload widening, arch-9 probe queue). Nothing in the Phase-1 artifact is structurally wrong or a ticking time bomb. The three-pillar architecture (code-IS-graph / content-addressed / DAG-bounded) holds coherently across 18,790 lines of Rust. R6-gate: PASS for progression to Phase-2 scope planning. Recommend landing finding arch-1 (drop benten-eval's benten-graph dep) as the first Phase-2 refactor since it's small, testable, and tightens the most important architectural boundary (evaluator-ignorance-of-storage).
